region_of_origin: Australia / New Zealand (common lamb-producing regions); consumed globally

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb loin chop entry; the grilled and lean-trimmed specificity is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 base term for sheep meat used; grilled preparation and lean-trim modifier applied at descriptor level. No exact FoodEx2 code for grilled lean lamb midloin chop exists in published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for grilled lean lamb midloin chop identified in published release.

serving.description: 1 lean grilled lamb midloin chop (bone-in, fat trimmed)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical lean grilled lamb midloin chop with bone, after trimming all visible fat, yields approximately 80–110 g of edible lean meat; 100 g is used as the reference serving for nutritional purposes.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, loin, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, cooked, broiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #17174; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo F06050010
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, dietary fibre) are taken from HPB Singapore Foodo entry F06050010 (confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed), which is an approved primary regional source. Fatty acid fractions (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Lamb, loin, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, cooked, broiled (NDB 17174), which is the closest approved-source equivalent for fatty acid detail not reported by HPB. Both are approved sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled lean lamb midloin chop contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Pure protein and fat foods are excluded from GI classification per University of Sydney GI Database methodology and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (74 mg/100 g) with high potassium (330 mg/100 g) yields a strongly favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary targets.
hypertension.guidance: Grilled lean lamb midloin chop is suitable for hypertension management when prepared without added salt or high-sodium marinades; its naturally low sodium and high potassium content support blood pressure control per DASH guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate (0.0 g/100 g) and zero sugar (0.0 g/100 g) produce no postprandial glycaemic response; high protein (29.8 g/100 g) supports satiety and lean mass preservation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled lean lamb midloin chop is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contributes no dietary carbohydrate or sugar, exerts no glycaemic load, and provides high-quality protein to support glycaemic stability; avoid sugary glazes or sauces that would add carbohydrate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 9.1 g/100 g with saturated fat approximately 3.8 g/100 g and ruminant trans fat approximately 0.2 g/100 g; cholesterol 92 mg/100 g; lean trimming substantially reduces saturated fat versus untrimmed cuts.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lean-trimmed grilled lamb midloin chop is a moderate cardiovascular concern; the lean preparation reduces saturated fat significantly compared with untrimmed lamb, but the residual saturated fat and ruminant trans fat warrant limiting portion frequency to 1–2 servings per week in line with AHA dietary guidance for cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid in red meat amplifies RA-relevant inflammatory cytokines; ruminant trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory; no anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids present in meaningful quantity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled lean lamb midloin chop is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content and ruminant trans fat; substitution with oily fish rich in EPA and DHA (salmon, mackerel, sardines) is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled lamb midloin chop, lean, is a bone-in cut taken from the loin section of a lamb carcass, grilled until fully cooked through with a lightly charred exterior, and trimmed of all visible external and internal separable fat before or after cooking. The lean muscle meat is tender, mildly gamey in flavour, and firm yet juicy in texture when properly grilled. Per 100 g of edible lean meat, it provides 201 kcal, 29.8 g of high-quality complete protein, 9.1 g of total fat (approximately 3.8 g saturated, 0.2 g ruminant trans fat), 0.0 g carbohydrate, 0.0 g sugar, 74 mg sodium, 330 mg potassium, 11 mg calcium, 2.8 mg iron, and 92 mg cholesterol. The zero carbohydrate content makes it glycaemically inert, suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. The high potassium-to-sodium ratio supports DASH dietary targets for blood pressure management. The residual saturated fat and ruminant trans fat warrant moderate consumption frequency for individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis. Lamb is halal when slaughtered according to Islamic rites, making this cut widely consumed across Muslim communities in Singapore, Malaysia, and the Middle East. It is commonly served as a main protein in Western, Middle Eastern, and mamak-style restaurant contexts. The cut is classified as minimally processed — the only transformations are fat trimming and dry-heat grilling without added ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lean grilled lamb chop?
Is lamb chop suitable for diabetics?
Protein content of grilled lamb midloin chop
Is lamb chop good for high blood pressure?
Can I eat lamb chop on a keto diet?
Is lamb chop high in cholesterol?
Lean lamb chop vs fatty lamb chop nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06050010, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed), which is an approved primary regional source. Fatty acid fractions supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Lamb, loin, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, cooked, broiled (NDB 17174), an approved secondary global source. Both sources are explicitly approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero (0.0 g/100 g) per HPB Foodo source — this is naturally zero as lamb is a pure animal protein and fat food with no carbohydrate content. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a muscle-meat product with no plant-derived fibre. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas protocol. Saturated_fat_g (3.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.7 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6 g), and trans_fat_g (0.2 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17174 (Lamb, loin, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, cooked, broiled) as HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid fractions for this entry; this substitution is documented in nutrient_source. Fields vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB entry are not fully reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026; USDA SR Legacy entry 17174 was consulted for fatty acid fractions but full micronutrient mapping was not applied to avoid over-attribution. The input cultural.primary_cuisine was tagged as mamak; this has been preserved in meal_context but the cuisine field is set to Generic / Western as grilled lamb midloin chop is not exclusively a mamak dish and the generic record type warrants a broader classification. Halal dietary flag set to true consistent with input dietary_tags. Ruminant trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) is naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid from ruminant fat, distinct from industrially produced trans fat, but is noted as pro-inflammatory in the rheumatoid arthritis health context per published literature. GI source citation updated to reference University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) to satisfy G2 approved-source compliance for GI evidence.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is the precise canonical English name from HPB Foodo. This is a generic Western food item, not a Singaporean dish, so no mandatory local-language names are required under G6. Identity is unambiguous.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 201.0, protein_g 29.8, fat_g 9.1, carbohydrate_g 0.0 all present and numeric. Carbohydrate zero is justified as naturally zero for a pure muscle-meat product. Primary source is HPB Singapore Foodo (approved primary regional source, exact match, confidence 0.95, reviewed). Fatty acid fractions from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17174 (approved secondary global source). nutrient_source fully populated with both approved sources named and URLs provided.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (naturally zero, muscle meat), sodium_mg 74.0, saturated_fat_g 3.8 (from USDA SR Legacy NDB 17174, documented), sugar_g 0.0 (naturally zero). All four required secondary fields present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) as food contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source fully populated referencing University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) — both are approved GI sources under G2. Compliant with the zero-carbohydrate GI rule.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is Type 2 Diabetes. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis included from input.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains 6 English-language variants covering the canonical name, alternate orderings, and common abbreviations. aliases_by_language english array mirrors all flat alias entries exactly. This is a generic Western food item with no mandatory local-language aliases required. All strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — consistency check passed.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'close' to 'closest_parent'.

Iteration 3: Fixed G2 approved-source compliance — nutrient_source title and organization updated to explicitly name both HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as approved sources with correct URLs. GI source updated to reference University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) to satisfy G2 for GI evidence. glycemic_index confirmed as null (numeric-null, not string) to satisfy Q4 requirement that GI be present in a valid form for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

GI source updated to cite University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed journal, satisfying G2 for GI evidence. glycemic_index is null per protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Core nutrients sourced from HPB Foodo (exact match, reviewed). Fatty acid fractions from USDA SR Legacy 17174 with full documentation. Zero carbohydrate, sugar, and fibre justified as naturally zero. All three mandatory health conditions present with clinical guidance. Ontology anchors defensible with honest gap notes. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Alias consistency verified.
